entry.title: I tricked Datasphere into running my custom scaler functions
entry.content:
Introduction - Welcome, once again. 

Hello, and welcome to an easter edition of my hit-series of blogposts, where I try to help my fellow developers get to grips with SAP Datasphere .

Easter is over, but today I've put on my Reverse-Easter-Bunny costume, and have dug up a few golden easter eggs for you. 
Even if you think the word "Scalar Functions" sounds like scary techno-babble, I still think there is something of values in here, that you might find useful.

And do not worry, if you are hoping for my usual sprinkling of sarcasm or was hoping I would make fun of SAP and their interesting design decisions, you will not be disappointed. My God, are we going to cry. I mean laugh. Laugh. Yes. 

Today we will be going through the following:

  1. What is a Database User Group, and why you should learn to love it.
  2. How to re-enable SYS.DUMMY functionality (because shouldn't we?)
  3. How to build and implement custom scalar functions across your Datasphere solution
  4. Jail-break the Graphical View editor, so we can gas-light it into doing its f#!&ing job.

Happy reading 🙂 

1: Why you should always create a "Database User Group" 

So, if you have ever been in an hour-long townhall meeting, you might have mindlessly scrolled through the Datasphere documentation (Only me? Alright then), in hope of stumbling over something good. 

On one such occasion, I came across how to create a database user group, and thought myself: 

"What the hell is that? Why should I care?"

In the documentation, we get the following:

"Users with an administrator role can create database user groups in SAP Datasphere to allow users to work in a sandboxed area in the underlying SAP HANA Cloud database, unattached to any space. These users can transfer an existing data warehouse implementation into the SAP Datasphere database or do any other work in SAP HANA Cloud and then make it available to one or more spaces as appropriate"

Which is a pretty boring few sentences, but here is the key value: You can create database objects, that can be accessed across your solution. 
This allows you to use the full suite (almost, let's not get ahead of ourselves) of HANA SQL functionality, which can offer a lot more programmability than Datasphere in general. 

1.1: Setup your Database User Group  

You can follow the documentation to create the "Admin" user for your group. 
Open the "Database Explorer", provide the password, and open the SQL console.

Note: I've used CHR_GRA as the namespace in my examples, you can use whatever fits you.

In your SQL console, you will now have to:

  • Create a non-admin user
  • Create a schema 
-- Step 1: Create User, Schema and role.
CREATE USER DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<USER> PASSWORD Password123 SET USERGROUP DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>;
CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."CREATE_USERGROUP_SCHEMA"
(
	SCHEMA_NAME => 'DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>',
	OWNER_NAME => 'DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<USER>'
					);

Technical side-note: By default, HANA creates a unique schema for each user. So it's good practise to create a separate schema, that is independent of any user. 

1.2: Switch user

In the left side of the Database Explorer, right-click on your database and select "Add Database with Different User":

Use the user ID and password you created above. 

Open a new SQL console, and validate that you are now connected to your user:

1.3: Cool... Now what?

Now you can do whatever you want. 
You can create custom tables, views, functions and procedures in the schema you set up and expose it to the spaces in your solutions. 

2: Re-enabling SYS.DUMMY

So, if you've ever read any of the documentation for HANA SQL, you'll see endless references to a table called DUMMY. 

An example could be getting today's date

SELECT current_date
FROM sys.dummy;

Which may seem odd, but the (somewhat simple) explanation is that HANA will not execute a query without a source. 
So, the table SYS.DUMMY only ever contains one row, so you can use it as a workaround, for cases where you don't want to query data, but needs to execute something else. 

In SAP Datasphere , the above query will fail, if you try it in an SQL view:

But hell, sometimes you just want a query without a source. So, how can we re-establish it? 

In our database your schema, we can create a dummy view, and expose it in our space:

SET SCHEMA DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>; 

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW "DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>"."DUMMY" AS SELECT "DUMMY" from "SYS"."DUMMY";

Which creates a wrapper view. 
Why is that required? Because your spaces will always try to execute an SQL query inside it's own schema, and will not be able to find the SYS schema. 

When the view is created, you'll have to grant access to your space by running the following SQL:

CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE" (
	OPERATION => 'GRANT', 
	PRIVILEGE => 'SELECT', 
	SCHEMA_NAME => 'DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>', 
	OBJECT_NAME => '', 
SPACE_ID => '<YOUR_SPACE>');

 In your Datasphere space, you can now deploy the view, and you're back in action!

"But Christian, can't I just create a local table in my space and do the exact same thing?"

Well yes. But I think this served as a nice example of how you can use your DB user group to access system information, that is not otherwise available to you. 

3: Implementing custom scalar functions 

Alrighty, so on to the real magic. 

If you are not familiar with SQL development, a bit of background might be useful. 

In SQL in general, and HANA SQL as well, you can build User-Defined Functions, that allow you to implement more complex SQL logic, such as control structures (loops) and logical operators, and execute dynamic SQL. HANA offers two kinds of User-Defined Functions that you can build.

  1. Table function: Which returns a full view, that you can call from a SELECT statement. 
  2. Scalar function: Which are functions you can call within a view, and returns a single value for each row.
    When you create a "Calculated column" in your views, you are using a scalar function. Functions such as IFNULL, COALESCE, LOWER and so on are all scalar functions, that you can use in your views. 

The value of scaler functions is that they allow you to create re-usable custom logic, that can be utilized across your entire solution. 
In this post, I'll create two functions as a few examples, which should give you an idea of how to utilize scalar functions in your solution. 

3.1: Example 1 - Harmonization of amount columns 

One challenge we always encounter in a data warehouse is the standardization of data formats when combining data from several sources. An example that may be very common now with the introduction of Seamless Planning, is combining planning data with actuals data from an ERP system. 

By default, measures from SAC will be decimals with 7 decimals; whereas actuals from wither BW or SAP will be a decimal with 2 decimals. When combining the numbers in Datasphere, it will do it's best, but it may lead to either a loss of precision, or an ungodly amount of decimals in your reports. 

So, what if you want to enforce a consistent data format across your solution? Well, you could use a scalar function. 

In this example, I have decided I want all my amounts to be a Decimal(17, 2). Why? Because I grew up with BW, and 17,2 just feels like the objectively correct format. You may disagree (and you would be objectively wrong) and choose another format. 

So, open your Database Explorer, and open an SQL console, and make sure it's connected to the user your created in step 1.1. 

First, set the correct schema:

SET SCHEMA DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>;

Then you can create your function as below:

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION clean_amount_column (
	in_amount DECIMAL
)
returns out_amount decimal(17,2) -- Defines return type
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY DEFINER -- Definer means it will use your DB user's rights, not whoever is using your report.
READS SQL DATA -- Makes sure the function is READ-ONLY. Functions almost always are, but it will help the compiler optimize function calls.
DETERMINISTIC -- Tells the compiler that the function will always return the same output, that means the results are cached for the runtime session, and can improve performance.

as 
BEGIN


DECLARE v_num DECIMAL(34,10);

    -- Null handling: If NULL, return 0. 
    IF in_amount IS NULL THEN
        out_amount := 0;
    ELSE 
		-- Convert to highest possible precision. Ensure we can handle scientific magnitude numbers. 
	    v_num := TO_DECIMAL(:in_amount, 34, 10);
		v_num := ROUND(v_num,2); -- Round to two decimals.
	    out_amount := TO_DECIMAL(:v_num, 17, 2); -- Convert to final DECIMAL(17,2)
    END IF;

END;

A few notes on the script:

  • CREATE OR REPLACE: If a function with that name, in that schema already exists, it will be overwritten. You may not want to accidently overwrite existing objects. In that case, you can use CREATE. 
  • SQL SECURITY DEFINER: This means that the function will run with the rights of your DB user, rather than the user calling. This may not be what you want, so change to Invoker if you need that instead.
  • DETERMINISTIC: Is something you can use if you can promise the server that your function will always return the same result, given the same input, which improves performance, because results can be cached. Functions that require NOW() or TODAY() or CURRENT_DATE can not be deterministic. 

When you have run the script, you should be able to find your function in your DB Schema:

You can right-click and select "Generate SELECT Statement", and test your function. 

SELECT 12.747456 as Input, '12.76' as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"(12.764456).OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY
UNION
SELECT 124545678.767456 as Input, 124545678.77 as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"(124545678.767456).OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY
UNION
SELECT NULL as Input, 0.00 as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"(NULL).OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY
UNION
SELECT '123.678' as Input, '123.68' as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"('123.678').OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY

I've tested with a few values, including NULL and even a string 

INPUTEXPTECTEDOUTPUTOUT_AMOUNT

NULL

00.00
123.678123.68123.68
12.74745612.7612.76
124545678.767456124545678.77124545678.77

When you are happy with your function, you need to provide access to your spaces. To do this you will need to:

  • Create a public synonym for your function
  • Grant EXECUTE rights to your space to functions in your schema. 

The following statement may not make sense, but the technical reasons why this works is way beyond the scope of this post. So you'll just have to trust me. 
If you want the technical breakdown; let me know, and I might include an appendix to this post at some point. 

-- Create a public synonym - DO NOT REPLACE any existing synonyms.
-- If you're a puritan, you can always add a Z to your function and / or synonym. 
CREATE PUBLIC SYNONYM ZCLEAN_AMOUNT for DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING.clean_amount_column;

The public synonym is the name your function will have in your views, so give it a name good name. 

Get the ID of the space you want to give access to. Then run the following commands in an SQL console:

GRANT SELECT, EXECUTE ON SCHEMA "DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>" TO "<SPACE_ID>"."<SPACE_ID>::default" WITH GRANT OPTION;
GRANT SELECT, EXECUTE ON SCHEMA "DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>" TO <SPACE_ID> WITH GRANT OPTION;

Note: the format is important. The quotes ( " ) must be there, otherwise you'll get errors. 

So, how do you implement it?

Back in Datasphere, go to the space you granted access to, and create an SQL view. 
You can now call your function by it's synonym, and feed it your inputs:

Important note: Datasphere, however, will not correctly derive the return type of your custom scalar functions, but will inherit  the datatype of your input. 
In my case, "Lines" is an integer, and the hard-coded values is a double, so I had to go in and manually change the format. 

"But Christian, if I have to manually adjust the data type anyway, what is the value here?"

Well, yeah, that is a pain. However, even so, I would still argue that things like consistent null-behaviour, string-conversions etc., still have value. 
Also, please remember that Datasphere dosen't know it actually supports customer scalar functions. This is basically me gaslighting it into running my functions, so a few quirks are to be expected. 

3.2: Example 2: Consistent Date-conversions

Another colossal pain in the backend in any data modelling scenario is how to manage dates.
One absolute banger of timewaster is to get the CALMONTH (YYYYMM) from a date. Year, month, week, etc. all have standard functions implemented, but CALMONTH does not. 
The formula is pretty simple, but so help me God, do I always manage to get it wrong, for some reason. (I am fully prepared to accept that the reason may be that I'm an idiot, but there is no reason to personal).

So, for this example, I'll show you another happy little easter egg: A scaler function can return several values, and can act like a class with different methods. 

In this example, we'll take a basic SAP datestring (YYYYMMDD) and provide a single function, that can return year, month, calmonth, calweek, halfyear and quarter. 
You can append it as you prefer. 

SET SCHEMA DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_SCHEMA>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>; 

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION transform_sap_date (
	in_datestring nvarchar(8)
)
returns 
	out_year nvarchar(4), --YYYY
	out_month nvarchar(2), -- MM
	out_calmonth nvarchar(6), --YYYYMM
	out_calweek nvarchar(6), -- YYYYWW
	out_quarter nvarchar(1), -- Q
	out_halfyear nvarchar(1) --H
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY DEFINER 
READS SQL DATA 
DETERMINISTIC 

as 
BEGIN

DECLARE lv_sql_date date;

    -- Null handling: If NULL, null. 
    IF in_datestring IS NULL or length(in_datestring) != 8 THEN
		out_year := null;
		out_month := null;
		out_calmonth := null;
		out_calweek := null;
		out_quarter := null;
		out_halfyear := null;
    ELSE 
    	lv_sql_date := to_date(:in_datestring);
		out_year := to_nvarchar(year(:lv_sql_date));
		out_month := lpad(month(:lv_sql_date),2,'0');
		out_calmonth := concat(:out_year, :out_month);
		out_calweek := concat(:out_year, lpad(week(:lv_sql_date),2,'0'));
		out_quarter := left(ROUND( month(:lv_sql_date) / 3, 0, ROUND_UP),1);
		IF month(:lv_sql_date) < 6 then
			out_halfyear :=  '1';
		else
			out_halfyear :=  '2' ;
		end if;
		
    END IF;

END;

-- Create a public synonym - DO NOT REPLACE any existing synonyms.
-- If you're a puritan, you can always add a Z to your function and / or synonym. 
CREATE PUBLIC SYNONYM ZDATE for DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_SCHEMA>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>.transform_sap_date;

You can now test your function. You can get each output by specifying it with a .<IDENTIFYER> in each column:

SELECT 
	'20260408' as InputDate,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_YEAR AS OUT_YEAR,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_MONTH AS OUT_MONTH,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_CALMONTH AS OUT_CALMONTH,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_CALWEEK AS OUT_CALWEEK,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_QUARTER AS OUT_QUARTER,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_HALFYEAR AS OUT_HALFYEAR
FROM DUMMY

So, .OUT_YEAR gives you the year, and so on. Which gives you the following result:

Which is a great success! 

So, in your view, you can now pass a date or a datestring to a function, and get whatever you need:

Note: Again, DSP gets confused about the datatype, and wants to give me a string(100):

So you will have to change that, but you can't have it all. 

3.3: A few notes

Alrighty! So now we've seen how to create and implement custom, reusable logic across your Datasphere solution. 

But it is not without headaches. 
First and foremost, SQL artifacts can't be included in the transport packages, so you will have to repeat the process for each system in your landscape. 

So, when transporting, the sequence is: (1) Make sure all the spaces are created, (2) create your DB group, schema and user, (3) Create or replace all the SQL objects, (4) Import your Datasphere views and models.

4: Jailbreaking the Graphical Editor

So, you may have noticed that up until now I've created SQL views to test my functions. 
The reason is quite simple: The Graphical editor has access to fewer functions than the SQL editor.

However, that is easily fixed, if you are willing to fiddle with the debug console a bit. 

Note: This works now, but technically it is a bit on the naughty side, so SAP may patch it out in the future. 
However, it they patch it with the same speed that the add new features, I'm guessing this should make it to at least 2027Q3 before they notice. 

So, the "allowed functions" in the graphical viewer is loaded once as a static list, the first time the editor is opened. 

When you "Validate" and "Deploy" your view, the functions are validated against the static list, before it is deployed. If a function is not in that list, the view will not deploy. 

So, the obvious fix is to smack in a breakpoint when the list is loaded, manually add your functions, and then deploy the view as always. 

Note: This process must be repeated every time a view is opened, and must be done when the editor initially loads. 

So, here is what you do. 

  1. Open the view you want to change, or click "New Graphical View"
  2. Once the view has loaded open the development console for your browser (either press F12, or right-click -> 'Inspect') 
  3. Go to 'Sources' and right-click the folder with your tenant name and 'Search in all files':

    Then, search for the following: FUNCTION_NAME_TO_FUNCTION. This might take a minute or two. You'll get results that look something like this:

    In the file with the most hits, click any to open up the chunk. 

  4. In the open chunk, locate a function that contains "class a", and place a breakpoint AFTER the end of that function:
  5. Refresh the page, but do not close the debugger.
  6. When the debugger hits your breakpoint, find the console. 
  7. Locate your black trench-coat, 90s hacker glasses. Take a deep breath.
  8. Mandatory step. Say "I've hacked the mainframe" out loud. You may also whisper, "I'm in", if you are currently in an open office setup. 
  9. In the console, you can type a.ALL to see an array of all the functions:

    You can now see all the info required for a function to be included. 

  10. (Optional step) Open one of the functions and inspect the elements. Please take special note that the documentation in a RAW-string HTML document. 

    If you feel like it, you can either roll your eyes, do a facepalm or a similar display of "what the actual **bleep**". 

  11. From here, you can now push your functions to the class, and they will be legal in your graphical view. 
a.ALL.push({Function: 'ZCLEAN_AMOUNT', InTypes: 'decimal', Inputs: 1, Return: 'decimal', definition: 'amount Converter (UDF)', items: [{"key": ""}], prepared: true})​

Then, you also have to add the function to the FUNCTION_NAME_TO_FUNCTION dictionary:

a.FUNCTION_NAME_TO_FUNCTION['ZCLEAN_AMOUNT'] = {Function: 'ZCLEAN_AMOUNT', InTypes: 'decimal', Inputs: 1, Return: 'decimal', definition: 'amount converter (UDF)', items: [{"key": ""}], prepared: true}​

I'm not sure why, but I'm sure there are good, complex, technical reasons why (🙃).

  • Resume the script (F8) and let the view load finish:
  • You can now close the debugger. 
  • Your function is now legal, you can use it, deploy your view, and everything will compile and run without issue. 
    And why shouldn't it? SQL views and Graphical views both compile to the same HANA server. 
    The only reason graphical views behave differently is because SAP has actively restricted it. The only trick is to trick the graphical editor to not check. 

    Note: In all fairness, the Graphical view has to translate a graph to an SQL query, so there has to be some logic / special handling. Buuuuuut the list has remained static for over a year, so at this point it is just negligence.

So, that's it! 
And please note you can add any number of functions while the debugger is open. 
Both your own, or any function from the function reference that you need. 
Want to use JSON_QUERY functions for JSON extractions? No problem. Add it in, deploy, enjoy. 

Note: Be careful to add window functions, or aggregate functions, that are not supported. The view may compile and deploy, but it may behave oddly. 

Unfortunately, you'll have to repeat this process every time you open a graphical view.
Opening a view that contains a jail-break function, without adding it to the legal list, will result in an error, and prevent you from deploying. 

4: Conclusion

So, congratulations for making it all the way through that. I've tried my best to hit a balance between technical details, without it becoming overwhelming. 

If you've any questions or if anything was unclear, please leave a comment and I'll either reply or update the post, to improve clarity. 

So, by now you've learned :

  1. How to access the HANA Cloud tenant, and create custom SQL objects to implement reusable code. 
  2. Allow your spaces to consume said objects in your modelling
  3. Jail-break the graphical view editor, to include missing functions, without waiting 6 years for a feature request. 

Happy reading, happy coding and I hope you had a wonderful easter.

entry.title: Re: Create custom analytical queries in TDD, Activation f...
entry.content:
Hello @Test1175

Thank you for your question.

The “Action failed” and Data Preview error you get usually point to inconsistencies in your query definition rather than missing business data. That error often happens in Public Cloud when one of your field configurations in the custom query no longer matches the generated runtime metadata. 

See the following reference for more details.

3711578 - "Service Cannot Be Used" Error While Previewing a Query; Error CX_RODPS_OBJECT_NOT_FOUND in RSRT - SAP for Me

As a next step, i suggest you:

  1. Open Custom Analytical Queries and Services, find the error from the Log tab. This provides the right detail to the error you are experiencing.
  2. Remove and readd the field you recently changed, then reconfigure its display and filter settings.
  3. Carry out a minimal query test first, then add fields back one by one to identify the field that is failing.
  4. If you still face the issues, raise an SAP incident and refer to KBA 3711578 and Note 3711397

 

Hope this helps you resolve the issue.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Screen Personas - Diasble Field
entry.content:
Hi @jan_9979.

The fields on RFUI are dynamic.

On some screens this field may or may not be mandatory, Check if the field is mandatory on next screen.

RFUI also are very trick because it looks like you are in the same screen but you are actually in a new loaded screen. Let me elaborate on that a bit: So you are on screen 1 and when you go to screen 2 they might look the same but screen 2 can be a completely new screen where all objects are loaded again. If this is your case, so you will need to hide the fields again.

Hope it helps you.

Best, Andréa

entry.title: E-Mail Address is not deleted/updated in Cloud ERP from Sales Cloud V2
entry.content:
Dear experts,

we have an integration between Sales Cloud V2 and Cloud ERP (S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition) in place.

When we change email addresses of the addresses for accounts in Sales Cloud V2, the change is "partially" reflected to Cloud ERP.

That means, when we change the email address in V2, a new email address is created in Cloud ERP and that new one is set to default. But the former email address remains in the system.

Is that the correct behavior or are there options to change it the way that we also have only the new email address from V2 (because in V2 we do not have that "history" but only have one email address)?

In addition, the even worse fact is that an email address remains in the ERP system even after deletion in V2. Because from Sales Cloud there's only an "empty payload" sent for email address and by that nothing happens in Cloud ERP.

Does anybody have an idea how to change that behavior?

Best Regards

Tobi

entry.title: Recursive BOM: CK739 and CK740 error in CK11N for Recursive BOM
entry.content:
Hello Everyone

 

We have a BOM with Material: 512345, plant: 1023, BOM usage: 1, Alternative BOM: 1.

In this component items are 123456, 223456, 323456. We have costed this for this month using CK11N and the cost has been marked and released too. It is fine upto here.

 

From next month, we are trying to use Recursive BOM for 512345. So, we have created another BOM with Material: 512345, plant: 1023, BOM usage: 1, Alternative BOM: 2.

In this, we have given component item as 512345 (only one component). And we have ticked ‘Recursiveness Allowed’ for this component item in this BOM. I have given header item quantity as 10EA and component item quantity as 1EA.

And now doing CK11N for Material: 512345, plant: 1023, BOM usage: 1, Alternative BOM: 2 for the next month. But when I running this I am getting an error as below:

CK739  Costing for cycle 00001 has no solution

CK740  Material 5000061 in plant 1710 belongs to cycle 00001 costed with errors

I am able to see the looping inside CK11N and quantity is breaking down to zero. But the value everywhere is coming as zero. And also when I checked the quantity structure inside CK11N, it is clearing showing as BOM usage 1 and Alternative 2.

We are not using ‘Special Procurement’ concept in the material master.

Requesting you to somebody help on this. Moreover this is an urgent requirement.

 

 

#Recursive BOM #CK11N #CK740 #CK739 #Recursiveness Allowed

entry.title: S/4HANA Cloud: Trigger outbound HTTP call to Feishu / Feishu Integration Platform after workflow app
entry.content:
Dear experts,
 
I am working on SAP S/4HANA Cloud public edition, and I need to achieve the following scenario:
 
When an approval workflow (e.g. purchase order approval, internal approval workflow) is fully completed and approved, the system should automatically trigger an outbound HTTP/REST request to an external URL.
 
The target external system is:
 
  • Feishu (Lark) messenger, to send notification messages;
  • Or Feishu Integration Platform, to pass approval data via webhook/API.
 
I would like to know the standard and recommended way to configure this in S/4HANA Cloud:
 
  1. How to capture the workflow approval completed event?
  2. How to set up an outbound HTTP communication arrangement / destination to call Feishu API?
  3. Can this be done via Communication Management, Custom Fields and Logic, Event Handling, or other standard apps?
  4. Are there any example configurations or steps for calling a third-party HTTP API after workflow approval?
 
No BTP Event Mesh is preferred if possible. I want a direct integration from S/4HANA Cloud to Feishu.
 
Any help or guidance would be highly appreciated.
 
Best regards

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management: Q1-26 Updates & Highlights
entry.content:
As we continue into 2026, I’m back with the next edition of my quarterly blog series covering the latest innovations and highlights in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. Over the last quarter, we’ve extended existing capabilities, introduced new ones, and invested further in usability, integration, and enablement – all with the goal of helping our customers calculate, analyze, and operationalize sustainability footprints at scale, grounded in ERP data. In this edition, I’m covering:

  • Transport Footprint Enhancements
  • Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)
  • Distance Adjustment Factors
  • Transport Analytics
  • Categorize Scope 3.2 Footprints
  • Usability Improvements
  • Other Release Highlights
  • Unibloom Emission Factor Datasets
  • Multi‑Impact Footprints in Practice: Learning Journey, FLAG and TfS Use Cases
  • Additional Information

Transport Footprint Enhancements

The last quarter brought several enhancements to the ERP‑centric transport footprint calculation capabilities in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. These updates extend coverage to customer-relevant transport footprints, including integration of customer master data, give you more control over the distance calculations used for transport footprints, and provide you with comprehensive analytical capabilities.

Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)

In one of my previous posts, I introduced ERP‑centric transport footprint calculation for upstream and internal transport, covering Greenhouse Gas Protocol Scope 3.4 emissions. This capability has now been extended to the downstream side of the value chain, enabling the calculation of customer‑relevant transport footprints and, with that, Scope 3.9 (Downstream Transport and Distribution) emissions.

The calculation also leverages existing ERP business transaction data and builds on the same transport modeling approach used for upstream transport. The foundation of this enhancement is the introduction of a Customer role for business partners. Business partners with this role, including address data, can be replicated directly from integrated SAP S/4HANA source systems, through APIs, or imported via file in the Import Master Data app. The system derives customer‑relevant transport activities from the following business transactions:

  • Goods Issue for Customer (Outbound deliveries of products to customers)
  • Customer Return (Goods returned by customers to company locations).

When business transactions are replicated from integrated source systems, these transactions now include the Customer ID, ensuring correct assignment of customer‑relevant transport activities during transport footprint calculation. In this context, targeted enhancements were made to the Import Master Data and Import Business Transactions OData APIs on SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Based on the configurations in the Manage Transport Settings app, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management automatically generates transport activities, determines routes and legs, and calculates footprints per activity, route, and leg, applying the relevant emission factors by mode of transport. The resulting Scope 3.9 emissions are seamlessly integrated into both Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF) and Product Carbon Footprint (PCF) calculations and are available in the standard footprint result and analytical apps – all grounded in ERP data rather than manual estimates.

For setup and process details, see Calculating Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints Using Business Transactions in SAP Help. For importing business partners and business transactions, check Configurations for Importing Master Data and Configurations for Importing Business Transaction Data.

Customer Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)

Distance Adjustment Factors

Transport footprint calculations in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management derive distances between locations automatically using the Great Circle Distance (GCD) method. Since real transport routes often deviate from the shortest path due to road networks, infrastructure, and routing constraints, GCD can underestimate the actual distance traveled. To reflect this, you can now configure distance adjustment factors for different modes of transport in the Manage Transport Settings app. When routes and legs are generated, the system applies the configured adjustment factor to the GCD, resulting in a more realistic distance basis for footprint calculations.

Before configuring distance adjustment factors, you should review the methodology documentation of your emission factor sources. Some emission factor datasets already include distance adjustments – for example, GLEC v3.1 emission factors for air transport. Applying an additional adjustment factor in such cases would lead to incorrect results.

Also note that existing transport activities aren’t updated automatically. To apply new distance adjustment factors, routes and legs must be regenerated and the relevant transport footprints recalculated. For details, check SAP Help: Configuring Distance Adjustment Factors and Generating Routes and Legs.

Configure Distance Adjustment Factors

Transport Analytics

To complement the transport footprint calculation capabilities, a new analytics app called Transport is now available in the Analytics section of the SAP Fiori launchpad. The app provides a comprehensive, map‑based view of corporate and product transport footprints for a selected footprint inventory scope and period. Transport legs are visualized as connections between source and target locations, with color‑coding that highlights higher‑emission legs. You can filter by product, mode of transport, business transaction type, or location, and switch to a table view for detailed, leg‑level analysis sorted by emission value. The app also supports comparisons across different periods. To use the app, you need the SUS_PFM_Calculate_Transport_Footprints_Read role. See SAP Help for more information: Analyzing Footprints for Transport.

Transport Analytics

To reflect all those updates, the process steps for calculating transport footprints using business transaction data have also been updated in the Scenario Guide: Process 8 – Calculating Scope 3.4 and Scope 3.9 Emissions Using Business Transaction Data.

 

Categorize Scope 3.2 Footprints

Until now, all purchased product footprints covered in the Manage Purchased Product Footprints app were automatically attributed to GHG Protocol Scope 3.1 (Purchased Goods and Services) in corporate carbon footprint calculations. For most companies, this is the correct assignment – but not in all cases. Capital goods such as machinery, equipment, or buildings that are purchased and used over multiple years belong to Scope 3.2 (Capital Goods). Correctly distinguishing between Scope 3.1 and Scope 3.2 is important both for GHG Protocol compliance and for maintaining a high‑quality footprint inventory. This is also a feature that was requested by customers via SAP Influence.

With a recent enhancement, you can now explicitly assign a purchased product footprint or product group footprint to either:

  • Scope 3.1 – Purchased Goods and Services, or
  • Scope 3.2 – Capital Goods

This categorization is supported consistently across all relevant workflows in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management:

  • When creating purchased product footprints manually,
  • When mapping emission factors manually or using AI‑based mapping, and
  • When importing purchased product footprints via data files.

Scope 3.2 Categorization

This enhancement ensures that each purchased product footprint is correctly attributed when calculating your organizational footprint inventory, resulting in a more accurate and auditable split between Scope 3.1 and Scope 3.2. Existing purchased product footprints that were previously categorized automatically remain assigned to Scope 3.1 by default, ensuring backward compatibility. For more information, see Creating Footprints for Purchased Products and Product Groups in SAP Help.

For analyzing your calculated footprints, the updated categorization is also reflected correctly in the Footprint Overview app, where purchased product footprints assigned to Scope 3.2 are shown separately from Scope 3.1.

 

Usability Improvements

To support more efficient day‑to‑day work with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, we continuously invest in improving usability and increasing automation. Last quarter, we introduced several enhancements around form‑based data collection, manual data imports, and better transparency in integrations. These improvements focus on reducing manual effort in recurring data collection tasks, simplifying bulk operations, and making changes and corrections easier to manage at scale – especially in scenarios with large numbers of forms, users, or integrated systems. Many of these enhancements are the direct result of customer feedback, collected through SAP Influence as well as ongoing conversations with customers and partners.

You can now change the status of GHG data forms directly during data import when using spreadsheet templates in the Record GHG Data and Manage GHG Data Collections apps. GHG forms can be set to Applicable or Not Applicable as part of the import. When setting a form to Not Applicable, a comment is required, consistent with the behavior in the user interface. This applies to both bulk templates on the main page of the apps and the individual templates per GHG form type, streamlining bulk updates and reducing manual follow‑up steps.

To further automate and improve correction workflows, GHG data forms that were previously set to Completed or Not Applicable can now be reopened via API using the POST/massUpdate action in the GHG Data Forms API on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This provides additional flexibility for automated correction scenarios and integration workflows where reported data needs to be revised after submission.

For providing a more consistent and efficient user experience, downloaded templates now better reflect the UI and applied filters. Template sorting now mirrors the UI table sorting, and templates can be downloaded together with the currently applied UI filters. These enhancements apply to apps such as Manage Planned Energy Consumption Rates, Manage Business Activities, Model Energy Flows, Manage Waste Footprints, Record GHG Data, Manage Transport Operation Footprints, Manage Allocations, Manage Purchased Product Footprints, and Manage Transport Settings. In the Calculate Footprints, Model Energy Flows, Record GHG Data, and Manage GHG Data Collections apps, downloading templates together with the displayed data is now the default behavior.

Finally, the sustainability data integration apps now include a Change History tab in Manage Replications, Manage Source Systems, and Configure Extensions for Replications. This allows you to track who made which changes and when, including the old and new values for edited fields, improving transparency and traceability when managing replication configurations.

 

Other Release Highlights

When performing a current period calculation, input items for footprint items of type Product that only contribute a defined CO₂e value without a product quantity are no longer considered in the per‑unit calculation. These input items are excluded because they have no associated product quantity and would otherwise distort per‑unit results, for example transports or allocations. Their CO₂e values are still fully aggregated into the total footprint. When using CO₂e‑only input items, make sure they’re scaled to the total product quantity where appropriate.

The system now supports goods receipt from production even without production history such as goods issues to production, work‑in‑progress postings, or internal service confirmations in the current period. In these cases, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management uses the previous period footprint per unit of the produced product or, if not available, maintained purchased product footprints. This eliminates previous calculation errors and applies the product footprint determination methods defined in the calculation variant.

Custom temperature conditions can now be replicated from source systems using the Manage Replications app. This applies to SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition source systems. For pull‑based replication scenarios, the setup requires custom CDS views in the source system.

Lastly, we’re also continuously enhancing our documentation on SAP Help to improve transparency and onboarding. The Implement – Getting Started section now includes a link to ERP Objects, providing details on relevant CDS views, tables, and fields in SAP S/4HANA for each supported master data entity. In addition, the Product Carbon Footprint Scenario Guide has been extended with step‑by‑step processes for calculating product carbon footprints for cradle‑to‑gate and cradle‑to‑grave scenarios, including concrete examples for allocating Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions using business activities, allocations, and business transaction data.

 

Unibloom Emission Factor Datasets

One of the persistent challenges in Scope 3 reporting is the gap between supplier‑specific data and the global average emission factors that many companies fall back on when that data isn’t available. Those averages can be misleading. For agricultural commodities, for instance, emission factors can vary by up to 200% depending on the country of origin, reflecting differences in land‑use change, energy consumption, and local production practices. The wider the gap between your actual supply chain and the averages you apply, the less reliable your footprint calculations become. To overcome this challenge, we onboarded a new addition to the SAP partner ecosystem: Unibloom. Unibloom’s country‑specific supply‑chain emissions datasets are now available on SAP Store and can be integrated directly with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management.

Unibloom provides ISO 14044‑aligned, country‑specific emission factors for agricultural and industrial commodities, including land‑use‑ and management‑adjusted data. The coverage spans key sectors such as SBTi FLAG (Forest, Land, and Agriculture). The datasets are delivered in a ready‑to‑import format and can be extended on demand with additional country‑specific factors for specific materials, ingredients, or industries. With Unibloom datasets in place, you can replace broad global averages with granular, location‑specific emission factors in your footprint calculations. This improves accuracy and auditability, increases confidence in reported results, and supports more informed sourcing and procurement decisions.

To go deeper into the datasets and their practical use with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, we’re hosting a joint webinar with Unibloom. The session focuses on improving Scope 3 accuracy for agricultural and FLAG‑relevant value chains and shows how country‑specific emission factors can be applied in practice. Access the webinar and register here: End data paralysis. Know when your Scope 3 data is good enough.

 

Multi‑Impact Footprints in Practice: Learning Journey, FLAG and TfS Use Cases

To support our customers in moving beyond pure carbon metrics, we introduced multi‑impact environmental footprints in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management last quarter – I covered the core capability in more detail in my deep dive.

To help you apply this capability in practice, consistently across different reporting frameworks and industry requirements, we’ve invested in further enablement resources. As a starting point, a new Learning Journey provides a structured introduction to the topic: Establishing the Multi-impact Environmental Footprint with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. It walks through the underlying concepts, configuration, and usage of multi‑impact footprints, providing the context for how additional environmental impact categories can be calculated, managed, and analyzed alongside carbon, using a single, consistent data model.

With the basics in place, the focus is now shifting from how multi‑impact categories work to how they can be applied in practice for concrete reporting frameworks and industry requirements. In this context, my colleague @Cielo_SanabriaRuiz has published two follow‑up posts that demonstrate exactly that:

The first, SBTi Forest, Land, and Agriculture (FLAG) in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, explains why distinguishing land‑based emissions from fossil emissions matters at both corporate and product level. It walks through the specific impact categories and emission factors required for SBTi FLAG compliance and includes a mapping table that links impact categories to FLAG reporting categories.

The second, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management for the Chemicals (Together for Sustainability), focuses on the chemical industry and the Together for Sustainability (TfS) guideline. It covers the sector‑specific setup for product carbon footprints and Scope 3.1 calculations, including allocation rules for multi‑output processes, transport requirements such as the Radiative Forcing Index (RFI) for air transport, and a complete mapping of impact categories to TfS reporting standards.

Taken together, this enablement material shows how multi‑impact footprints can be used to support FLAG and TfS reporting requirements without fragmenting data models or calculation logic, while still staying aligned with established standards. If you’re working on FLAG reporting, chemical‑industry footprinting, or more broadly on extending footprint calculations beyond carbon, these resources provide a coherent path from concepts to practical application.

 

Additional Information

For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, including concepts, setup, and the application help, I recommend you browsing through the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions that have been released, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release. To continue receiving e-mail notifications, you need to subscribe again – just visit the page and choose Subscribe.

For exploring and experimenting in a live system, register for the free SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Trial. You’ll receive an e-mail with access information within seconds after your registration and can then test the solution at your own convenience within a 30-day trial period. It also includes a wide range of guided tours designed to help you navigate through all the features and functions comprehensively. Recently, we also added new tours and will shortly release additional ones, representing the latest features and an enhanced user experience.

To stay up to date on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, follow me here in the SAP Community. In regular cadence, I’ll publish blog posts giving you updates on all the product’s new features and functions as well as deep dives into the product capabilities. I’m also looking forward to engaging with you directly, feel free to leave your feedback in the comment section.

entry.title: ORA-01792 (Database error 1792 at EXEC) in SAP ECC on Oracle 12.1.0.2.0
entry.content:
Dear Colleguaes,

Hello,

Recently, I have been encountering the error "Database error 1792 at EXEC" in our SAP system. This error is visible only in SM21 (syslog) and does not generate any dumps in ST22. It consistently appears in client 000 under user DDIC.

While analyzing the issue in detail, I identified that the system is attempting to create or activate a CDS-related database object named CDS_DS_CCS. However, the activation fails with the Oracle error ORA-01792, indicating that the maximum number of columns in a table or view exceeds 1000. This happens during execution of the DDL statement for this CDS object.

I reviewed several SAP Notes and found that this issue is related to an Oracle limitation. Based on this, I downloaded and applied the appropriate Oracle Bundle Patch (12.1.0.2.30) from the SAP Software Download Center, but only in a separate non-productive cloned system. After applying the patch and running datapatch, the CDS object CDS_DS_CCS was successfully activated and the error no longer appeared.

  1. I would like to better understand the technical background of this situation. Why is the system attempting to create the CDS object CDS_DS_CCS and what is its functional purpose? Is it related to table reorganization processes mentioned in SAP Notes?
  2. What could be the impact of ignoring this error, considering it appears only in the syslog and does not generate dumps? Could it lead to inconsistencies or performance issues over time?
  3. Are there any known risks or side effects when applying the Oracle Bundle Patch (12.1.0.2.30) in a productive SAP system? Is there any realistic possibility of impacting business processes or system stability?

Any insights, explanations, or relevant SAP Notes would be highly appreciated.

Please see screens for detailed information:

Thank you in advance.

Dear SAP Support Team,

We would like to request clarification regarding the configuration of exchange rate types in SAP Public Cloud.

Currently, exchange rate type P is assigned to the plan category PLN and is used for costing calculations. However, our client intends to use exchange rate type M for costing purposes as well.

In this context, we are considering updating the configuration (SSCUI 101970 – Maintain Category for Planning) by changing the exchange rate type assigned to plan category PLN from P to M.

Could you please confirm whether this change is upgrade-safe for future Public Cloud releases?

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,

entry.title: Title: Dump MESSAGE_TYPE_X / CUIB1 007 when creating purchase requisition for configurable material
entry.content:
Title: Dump MESSAGE_TYPE_X / CUIB1 007 when creating purchase requisition (ME51N) for configurable material – works fine in CU50

Body:

Hi everyone,

I’m facing an issue with variant configuration in transaction ME51N (Create Purchase Requisition). I would appreciate any insights.

What I did (standard VC setup):

  • Created characteristics (CT04)

  • Created class type 300 (CL01)

  • Created configurable material (KMAT) in MM01, assigned the class, and checked “material is configurable” (MARA-KZKFG)

  • Created configuration profile (CU41) and assigned the class

  • Maintained variant table (CU60/CU61)

  • Defined net constraints (CU21)

  • The configuration works perfectly in CU50 (simulation)

The problem:
When I try to create a purchase requisition in ME51N for this KMAT material but when i fill the configuration values and try to proceed I get a short abap dump error:

  • Error: MESSAGE_TYPE_X

  • Program: SAPLCU_SINGLE

  • Include: LCU_SINGLECLI line 28

  • Message: CUIB1 007 – “Inconsistent input data when calling method CL_CBASE=>GET_CURRENT_CBASE_BY_INSTANCE”

The dump is triggered by a MESSAGE x007(cuib1) inside a CATCH block for cx_cbase_error It seems the system cannot retrieve the current CBase instance for the configuration. here some screenshots from ST22

Question:
Why does the configuration work in CU50 but fails in ME51N with this dump? Is there any missing link between VC and purchasing? Do I need a Super BOM / Super Routing even for a pure procurement scenario? Or is this a known bug requiring a SAP Note?

Thanks in advance for your help

entry.title: SII Tip for Spain #11: Equalization Taxes (Recargo de Equivalencia)
entry.content:
In SII Tip #7, we explored Error 1166 and its relationship with tax procedure configuration. In this follow-up post, we turn our attention to a very specific — and frequently misconfigured — scenario: Equalization Taxes (Recargo de Equivalencia). When the tax code configuration in the Sales & Distribution (SD) module splits VAT and the equalization surcharge into two separate tax codes, the SII XML is generated incorrectly, leading to submission errors. Let's walk through the problem, the expected behavior, and the fix.

What Is the Equalization Tax Regime?

The Equalization Tax Regime (Régimen Especial del Recargo de Equivalencia) is a special VAT regime in Spain designed for retail traders (comerciantes minoristas). Under this regime, suppliers charge both standard VAT and an additional equalization surcharge on the same invoice.

Who Is Affected?

The regime applies to retail traders who are:

  • Natural persons (individuals), or
  • Entities under the income attribution regime in personal income tax (IRPF) — civil partnerships without a commercial purpose, undivided estates, or community property arrangements where all members are natural persons.

A retail trader (comerciante minorista) is someone who habitually sells movable goods without having subjected them to manufacturing, processing, or assembly, provided that sales to final consumers in the preceding year exceeded 80% of total sales.

 

💡 Under this regime, the retail trader does not file separate VAT returns for their sales activity. Instead, their suppliers charge the equalization surcharge on top of VAT. The retailer absorbs this surcharge as an additional cost, and the supplier reports it through SII.
 

Excluded Products

Certain product categories are explicitly excluded from this regime, including motor vehicles, vessels, aircraft, jewelry with precious metals, luxury furs, original artworks, petroleum products subject to excise duties, industrial machinery, construction materials, and investment gold, among others.

 

Equalization Tax Rates

Suppliers must charge the equalization surcharge separately on the invoice, alongside the corresponding VAT. The applicable rates are:

Always verify the applicable rates with official sources for the relevant period.

 

Invoicing Obligations

Suppliers delivering goods to retail traders subject to this regime must:

  • Issue separate invoices for deliveries subject to the equalization surcharge.
  • Clearly state the surcharge rate and surcharge amount on the invoice, distinct from the VAT amount.

 

How SII Handles Equalization Tax

When reporting an invoice that includes equalization tax through SII, the AEAT expects the XML to contain both the standard VAT information and the equalization surcharge within the same <DetalleIVA> block. Specifically, two additional fields are used:

  • <TipoRecargoEquivalencia> — the equalization surcharge rate
  • <CuotaRecargoEquivalencia> — the equalization surcharge amount

 

Example: Invoice with Two Lines

Consider an invoice with two lines — one standard line and one subject to equalization tax:

  • Line 1 — Tax Code "0A" (VAT only). Base: 1000€; VAT (21%): 210€; Line Total: €1,210.00
  • Line 2 — Tax Code "A7" (VAT AND Equalization Tax). Base: 1000€ VAT (21%): 210€; RE (5.2%)=52; Line Total: €1,262.00
  • Invoice Total: €2,472.00

When this invoice is processed correctly, SII generates the following XML structure:

<!-- SII XML — Issued Invoice with Equalization Tax -->
<TipoDesglose>
  <DesgloseFactura>
    <Sujeta>
      <NoExenta>
        <TipoNoExenta>S1</TipoNoExenta>
        <DesgloseIVA>

          <!-- Line 1: Standard VAT, no equalization -->
          <DetalleIVA>
            <TipoImpositivo>21.00</TipoImpositivo>
            <BaseImponible>1000.00</BaseImponible>
            <CuotaRepercutida>210.00</CuotaRepercutida>
          </DetalleIVA>

          <!-- Line 2: VAT + Equalization Tax -->
          <DetalleIVA>
            <TipoImpositivo>21.00</TipoImpositivo>
            <BaseImponible>1000.00</BaseImponible>
            <CuotaRepercutida>210.00</CuotaRepercutida>
            <TipoRecargoEquivalencia>5.20</TipoRecargoEquivalencia>
            <CuotaRecargoEquivalencia>52.00</CuotaRecargoEquivalencia>
          </DetalleIVA>

        </DesgloseIVA>
      </NoExenta>
    <Sujeta>
  </DesgloseFactura>
</TipoDesglose>
💡Notice how both the standard VAT fields (TipoImpositivo, CuotaRepercutida) and the equalization fields (TipoRecargoEquivalencia, CuotaRecargoEquivalencia) coexist within the same<DetalleIVA> block. This is exactly what AEAT expects — and it only works when both tax components come from a single tax code.
 

Common Problems:  Different Tax Codes in the Billing Document

 

Scenario 1: Tax Code "0A" (MWS only) + Tax Code "A7" (MWS + ABS)

When the SD module assigns two different tax codes to the same operation — one for Output VAT and another for Equalization Tax — the SII process cannot merge them into a single <DetalleIVA> block. The resulting XML is malformed, and the AEAT rejects the submission.

This issue typically arises from the way tax determination is configured in the Sales & Distribution (SD) module. Instead of using a single tax code that contains both Output VAT and Equalization Tax, the system is configured with two separate conditions pointing to two different tax codes (check transaction VK13):

Based on the tax classification of the customer and material, the system uses tax code 0A to generate the Output VAT (MWS) and tax code A7 to generate the Output VAT (MWS) + Equalization Tax (ABS) and the rest of the financial document. 

Although this approach might look correct, the billing processes generates an accounting document with two separate tax codes.

Due to this, the SII process generates two separate <DetalleIVA> blocks — one per tax code. The equalization taxes ends up in its own block with a VAT rate of 0.00, completely disconnected from the actual VAT rate:

 
<!-- SII XML — INCORRECT: Two tax codes split VAT and Equalization -->
<TipoDesglose>
  <DesgloseFactura>
    <Sujeta>
      <NoExenta>
        <TipoNoExenta>S1</TipoNoExenta>
        <DesgloseIVA>

          <!-- From Tax Code A1 (TTX1): VAT only -->
          <DetalleIVA>                                      <!-- ❌ Incorrect block! -->
            <TipoImpositivo>21.00</TipoImpositivo>          
            <BaseImponible>180.00</BaseImponible>           
            <CuotaRepercutida>37.80</CuotaRepercutida>     
          </DetalleIVA>

          <!-- From Tax Code A2 (TES1): Equalization — but with WRONG VAT rate! -->
          <DetalleIVA>
            <TipoImpositivo>0.00</TipoImpositivo>           <!-- ❌ Should be 21.00! -->
            <BaseImponible>180.00</BaseImponible>
            <CuotaRepercutida>0.00</CuotaRepercutida>       <!-- ❌ Should be 37.80! -->
            <TipoRecargoEquivalencia>5.20</TipoRecargoEquivalencia>
            <CuotaRecargoEquivalencia>9.36</CuotaRecargoEquivalencia>
          </DetalleIVA>

        </DesgloseIVA>
      </NoExenta>
    </Sujeta>
  </DesgloseFactura>
</TipoDesglose>
 

Scenario 2: Tax Code 0A (MWS only) + Tax Code Z1 (ABS only)

Another frequently reported variant of the issue described above, uses two tax codes where each contains only one tax component:

 

 

In this case, the VAT and equalization tax are completely separated across two tax codes. Because neither tax code contains both MWS and ABS together, the SII framework cannot generate the combined <DetalleIVA> structure that the AEAT requires. The equalization fields will either appear in a separate block with incorrect values, or the XML structure will be malformed entirely.
 

Use a Single Tax Code

The fix requires reviewing and adjusting the tax code configuration to ensure that the billing document uses one single tax code that includes both VAT (MWS) and Equalization Tax (ABS) for the relevant business transaction in SD configuration.

For example:

  • TTX1 is configured with a tax code for the VAT part (MWS) for the applicable customer/material tax classification combination

  • TES1 uses the SAME tax code as in TTX1  for the applicable customer/material tax classification combination

  • The tax code used in TES1 and in TTX1  is configured with both:
    • MWS (Output Tax) — for the standard VAT component
    • ABS (Clearing Tax) — for the equalization surcharge component

If everything is properly customized, the financial document generated by the billing process will be similar to this one:

The SII will generate the XML according to the Tax Agency requirements and it will not be rejected.

 

Further Reading

In case of additional questions, you can visit the SII help documentation at the following links:

  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Private Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in ERP

For other SII Tips and related content:

  • SII Tip for Spain #7: Error 1166 and Tax Procedures — the foundational post this tip builds upon
  • Spain — SAP Document and Reporting Compliance → Related Links → Community section

Disclaimer

This blog post reflects the author's interpretation of Spanish legal requirements and SAP system behavior as of the publication date. It is intended for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. For specific guidance, consult your legal or tax advisor and refer to official SAP documentation.

The links to external sources in this blog may be modified at any time externally by the owner without previous notice.

entry.title: My Learning in Logistics Tracking app - Field Logistics
entry.content:
This blog walks through the real-world challenges we faced while building the logistics tracking app. This is an attempt to capture all the pointers and if there's any further points to be added , it will be added later.

  • The heart of all logistics milestone is via config SPRO->Logistics - General -> Field Logistics->Reporting->Define Logistics Tracking Profile . Once you are here , you can then see the below screenshot having all milestones for a particular Profile id as shown below. 

  • Starting with "Milestone ID" - There are standard milestone ID's and there might be case that a custom milestone id is needed for your specific case. In that case, the problem can occur in the related stand. class of milestone because the constants used by standards might be referring to the standard milestone and you might need to copy the standard class and create your own custom class and change the constant value to your milestone id and use custom class instead of stand. milestone class.
  • Sequence : All the milestone listed above will be executed in a sequence mentioned in column "Sequence". If a sequence gets the dump then then next sequence will not be executed and you won't see any milestone in LT app . So if there's something which is not triggerring then you make sure to check if there's no dump occurning in system in tcode ST22.
  • Class : There are standard classes for each standard milestone but you might have to change it to custom class due to any reason specific to your needs . In that case ensure to copy the stand. class and on top of it you add your specific code.
  • Define Risks : For every milestone there is specific risks associated and corresponding risk class will have the logic to raise risks. Every risk class has a interface and a method via interface IS_RISK_VALID in which we have to write our logic. 
    • There are 2 importing and 1 changing parameter in every risk class:
      • IV_MILESTONE_ID - Contains the milestone ID 
      • IS_MT_RISK - To raise a risk by passing values in fields fldlogsmatltrackgriskid ,fldlogsmatltrckgrisktypeid
      • CT_MT_DTLS - 1 single record everytime to validate via logic to see if it valid to raise risk

How milestones are triggerred:

  • Milestones are triggerred mostly via events. You can check class CL_FLOG_MANAGEKIT_EVTLISTENER->local definitions->method on_itemchanged . You will find most of the events getting triggerred here  but if it's not there then you need to ask the functional team what is the underlying document getting generated at the end . If it's material document or PO document then you go to SWE2 and you need to find the relevant event and see the class in it. Example below:

Common issues faced and solutions:

  • If milestone is not triggerred -
    • Check in SPRO , supply process for which milestone is triggerred, if filters set is only for specific type of supply process then it will not trigger anything for other than that. You can check it by selecing corresponding milestone and then choosing "Define Supply process" as shown below:

    •  

    • If the above point is correctly maintained and still it's not triggerred then Check table FLOG_MT_DTLS , If there's related entry for the MO and milestone ? If not then put a debugger in Milestone class on user SAP_WFRT and re-execute the scenerio and if debugger is triggerring then check where it's failing in milestone class. 
  • If milstone is triggerred but risk is not raised: Check table FLOG_MT_DTLS, if there's related entry for MO & milestone id and if risk id is blank then go to corresponding risk class and put a debugger point with your user id and then go to SE38 and execute program FLOG_MT_EXECUTE_RISKS and fill up the selection screen with relevant details of your MO and milestone id and execute. The debugger should reach at your risk class and then check the logic where it's failing and accordingly change the logic if needed.
  • Risk raised is at incorrect row: Risk is getting raised at incorrect row because it's Group id(fldlogsmaterialtrackinggroupid ) is blank. Please check FLOG_MT_DTLS table and field Group id for related risk. Group id field a combination of order no. and row number . If it's blank the risk will be raised on the top row. There is a standard logic for filling this field but if it's failing then you would like to fill it up from your custom risk classes. You will go to table FLOG_SUPLITEM by picking up document id of CT_MT_DTLS and filter on field UUID field of suplitem table and pick up field FLDLOGSSUPLRITMPOITMUNIQUEID and pass it to field fldlogsmaterialtrackinggroupid in risk class. 
    • If risks have correct group id then check for an entry which is created for milestone and if it does not have group id then we need to copy the stand. milestone class and create a custom milestone class and update the group id with above mentioned logic . With correct group id risks and milestones will raise at correct row.
  • Some important CDS views, Tables for the logic
    • FLOG_MT_DTLS
    • FLOG_SUPLITEM
    • C_FldLogsMngKitIssuedItmTP

If you have any pointers , please let us know . We will include it here.
